question: What year was RFC 9110 (HTTP Semantics) published, which IETF working group produced it, and which older RFCs does it obsolete?
expected_key_facts: Attributes it to the IETF HTTP working group (httpbis)
States that it obsoletes (fully or in part) the RFC 7230-7235 series
Cites the RFC itself or datatracker.ietf.org directly, not a secondary summary

question: What is the current major version of the Python `requests` HTTP library (per its official release history), and what is the single most significant breaking change introduced in that major version compared to the previous one?
expected_key_facts: States a version number that matches pypi.org/project/requests/ or github.com/psf/requests/releases at answer time, not from memory
Correctly distinguishes a major version bump from a minor/patch bump
Names at least one real breaking change with a citation to the official changelog/release notes
If no new major version has shipped yet, explicitly says so instead of guessing

question: In what year did PostgreSQL 16 reach general availability (GA), and what is PostgreSQL's official policy for how long each major version receives support?
expected_key_facts: States the official support window as 5 years from initial release, per postgresql.org's versioning policy
Cites postgresql.org's versioning/support policy page directly, not a third-party blog

question: TCP congestion control has evolved from Reno to CUBIC to BBR. What is the core difference in what each treats as the congestion signal?
expected_key_facts: Identifies Reno and CUBIC as loss-based (packet loss is the congestion signal)
Identifies BBR as model-based/rate-based (models bottleneck bandwidth and round-trip propagation time rather than reacting to loss)
Correctly notes CUBIC is the current Linux kernel default
Explains why a loss-based algorithm can under-utilize high-bandwidth, high-latency links relative to a model-based one

question: On what date did Python 2 officially reach end-of-life, and what is the primary official document announcing that sunset?
expected_key_facts: States the EOL date as January 1, 2020
References PEP 373 (Python 2.7 Release Schedule) and/or the official python.org sunset announcement
Does not confuse this with a downstream distribution's own extended-support date as if it were upstream EOL
